mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive analysis of primary fuels and conversion technologies covers thermonuclear and fission sources (A.1), renewable solar, wind, and biomass sources (A.2), fossil fuel dependence (A.3), devices for energy utilization (B), and energy conversion technologies (C).
Nuclear fission and fusion technologies for energy production directly address primary nuclear energy sources (A.1), nuclear fission and fusion reactors (B.9), and nuclear-powered generators (C.7).

Solar photovoltaics, wind power, hydropower, and biomass address recurring energy sources (A.2). Wind and water turbines (B.2), solar energy devices (B.10), battery storage (C.4), and electricity generation from renewable sources (C.7).

Nuclear physics covering atomic structure, radioactivity, proton-neutron configurations, and nuclear stability provides the scientific foundations for primary nuclear energy sources including fission and radioactivity (A.1).

Radioactivity covering nuclear fission, fusion, and radioisotopes with applications in energy production directly addresses primary nuclear energy sources (A.1) and devices for utilizing nuclear energy (B.9).
Thermodynamic laws governing energy conservation, entropy, heat conversion, and free energy underpin all devices utilizing heat energy (B.7) and energy conversion processes (C).
Engineering harnessing electromagnetism and transistor technology relates to devices utilizing magnetic and electrical energy (B.5) and energy conversion devices (C).
Physics of light including lasers and quantum optics connects to lamps and lighting devices (C.5) and optoelectronic devices such as semiconductor lasers and optical fibres (D.3).

Electromagnetic theory through Faraday, Tesla, and Maxwell covering motors, generators, and turbines directly addresses magnets and electromagnets (B.5.a), electric motors (B.5.b), and turbine-driven generators (C.7).

Practical applications in silicon chips, liquid crystal displays, and magnetic memory directly address solid-state devices including transistors and semiconductor diodes (D.2) and optoelectronic devices including liquid-crystal displays (D.3).
Fluid dynamics and hydrodynamics connect to wind and water turbines (B.2) and hydraulic devices (B.4.d).
Thermodynamics, activation energy, and reaction kinetics connect to devices utilizing chemical energy (B.8) and thermoelectric energy conversion (C).
Solar physics covering the Sun's energy output and impact on Earth directly relates to solar energy as a recurring energy source (A.2) and devices for utilizing solar energy (B.10).

Scientific development of atomic fission and thermonuclear fusion for weapons connects to primary energy sources from thermonuclear reaction and nuclear fission (A.1), though the focus is military rather than energy utilization.
Covers the Carnot cycle and practical applications in heat pumps, solar cells, and energy transition — relevant to heat energy devices and batteries/fuel cells.
Covers atomic structure and nuclear science including heavy water — relevant to thermonuclear and fission energy sources.
